---
title: "💧 Global Sanitation Access: A Crisis Hidden in Plain Sight"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
    page-layout: full
    css: |
      body { background-color: #ffffff; color: #1a1a2e; font-family: 'Segoe UI', sans-serif; }
      h1, h2, h3 { color: #1565c0; }
      .quarto-title { background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1565c0, #42a5f5); color: white; padding: 30px; border-radius: 10px; }
      .callout { border-left: 4px solid #1565c0; }
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false

# ── Core imports ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes,
    geom_map, geom_col, geom_point, geom_line, geom_smooth,
    geom_text, geom_hline,
    scale_fill_gradient, scale_fill_manual, scale_color_gradient,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous,
    labs, theme, theme_bw, theme_minimal,
    element_text, element_rect, element_line, element_blank,
    coord_flip, facet_wrap
)
from plotnine.data import world
from mizani.formatters import percent_format
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# ── Colour palette (blue-on-white) ──────────────────────────────────────────
BLUE_DARK   = "#1565c0"
BLUE_MID    = "#42a5f5"
BLUE_LIGHT  = "#bbdefb"
WHITE       = "#ffffff"
GREY_TEXT   = "#424242"

# ── Custom ggplot theme ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def blue_theme():
    return (
        theme_bw() +
        theme(
            plot_background  = element_rect(fill=WHITE,  color=WHITE),
            panel_background = element_rect(fill="#f0f7ff", color=BLUE_LIGHT),
            panel_grid_major = element_line(color=BLUE_LIGHT, size=0.5),
            panel_grid_minor = element_blank(),
            axis_text        = element_text(color=GREY_TEXT, size=9),
            axis_title       = element_text(color=BLUE_DARK, size=10, face="bold"),
            plot_title       = element_text(color=BLUE_DARK, size=13, face="bold"),
            plot_subtitle    = element_text(color=GREY_TEXT, size=9),
            legend_background= element_rect(fill=WHITE),
            legend_text      = element_text(color=GREY_TEXT, size=8),
            legend_title     = element_text(color=BLUE_DARK, size=9, face="bold"),
        )
    )
```

```{python}
#| label: load-data
#| echo: true

# ── 1. Load raw files with Polars ────────────────────────────────────────────
indicator_raw = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
metadata_raw  = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv", infer_schema_length=5000)

# ── 2. Keep only "Total" sex rows (avoids double-counting) ──────────────────
indicator = (
    indicator_raw
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "sanitation_pct", "time_period": "year"})
)

# ── 3. Select useful metadata columns ───────────────────────────────────────
metadata = (
    metadata_raw
    .select([
        "country", "alpha_3_code", "year",
        "Population, total",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)",
    ])
    .rename({
        "Population, total":                      "population",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":     "gdp_per_capita",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)":"life_expectancy",
    })
)

# ── 4. Join indicator + metadata on country + year ──────────────────────────
merged = indicator.join(metadata, on=["alpha_3_code", "year"], how="left")

# ── 5. Latest-year snapshot (one row per country) ───────────────────────────
latest = (
    merged
    .group_by("alpha_3_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("country").first(),
        pl.col("year").max().alias("latest_year"),
        pl.col("sanitation_pct").last().alias("sanitation_pct"),
        pl.col("gdp_per_capita").last().alias("gdp_per_capita"),
        pl.col("life_expectancy").last().alias("life_expectancy"),
        pl.col("population").last().alias("population"),
    ])
    .drop_nulls("sanitation_pct")
)

# ── 6. Regional groupings (UN macro-regions) ────────────────────────────────
region_map = {
    "AFG":"South Asia","BGD":"South Asia","IND":"South Asia","NPL":"South Asia",
    "PAK":"South Asia","LKA":"South Asia","BTN":"South Asia","MDV":"South Asia",
    "NGA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","ETH":"Sub-Saharan Africa","COD":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "TZA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","KEN":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MOZ":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "GHA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","UGA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","ZMB":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "MDG":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MWI":"Sub-Saharan Africa","SEN":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "ZWE":"Sub-Saharan Africa","CMR":"Sub-Saharan Africa","CIV":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "NER":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MLI":"Sub-Saharan Africa","BFA":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "GIN":"Sub-Saharan Africa","RWA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","SSD":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "CHN":"East Asia & Pacific","IDN":"East Asia & Pacific","PHL":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "VNM":"East Asia & Pacific","MMR":"East Asia & Pacific","KHM":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "LAO":"East Asia & Pacific","PNG":"East Asia & Pacific","FJI":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "BRA":"Latin America","MEX":"Latin America","COL":"Latin America","ARG":"Latin America",
    "PER":"Latin America","BOL":"Latin America","HTI":"Latin America","GTM":"Latin America",
    "HND":"Latin America","NIC":"Latin America","DOM":"Latin America",
    "USA":"High Income","GBR":"High Income","DEU":"High Income","FRA":"High Income",
    "JPN":"High Income","AUS":"High Income","CAN":"High Income","NOR":"High Income",
    "SWE":"High Income","CHE":"High Income","NLD":"High Income",
    "EGY":"Middle East & N. Africa","MAR":"Middle East & N. Africa",
    "DZA":"Middle East & N. Africa","IRQ":"Middle East & N. Africa",
    "YEM":"Middle East & N. Africa","SYR":"Middle East & N. Africa",
}

region_pl = pl.DataFrame({
    "alpha_3_code": list(region_map.keys()),
    "region":       list(region_map.values())
})

latest = latest.join(region_pl, on="alpha_3_code", how="left").with_columns(
    pl.col("region").fill_null("Other")
)

# ── 7. Convert to pandas for plotnine ───────────────────────────────────────
latest_pd  = latest.to_pandas()
merged_pd  = merged.to_pandas()

print(f"Countries in latest snapshot : {len(latest_pd)}")
print(f"Total indicator rows          : {len(indicator)}")
print(latest_pd[["country","sanitation_pct","gdp_per_capita","life_expectancy"]].describe().round(1))
```

---

## 🌍 Introduction

Access to basic **sanitation** — toilets, latrines, and proper sewage systems — is listed as **UN Sustainable Development Goal 6**. Yet, decades after this commitment, billions of people still lack it.

This report uses UNICEF data on the **proportion of populations with access to improved sanitation facilities** across countries and time. The story that emerges is striking:

- **Less than 30%** of people in some Sub-Saharan African nations have access to improved sanitation.
- There is a **strong link** between a country's wealth (GDP per capita) and its sanitation coverage.
- **Progress has been uneven** — some regions improved dramatically since 2000, others barely moved.
- Countries with **low sanitation** also tend to have **lower life expectancy**, underscoring that this is ultimately a *health and human rights issue*.

> *"Sanitation is more important than independence."* — Mahatma Gandhi

---

## 📊 Chart 1 — World Map: Sanitation Access by Country

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Proportion of population with access to improved sanitation facilities (latest available year). Darker blue = higher coverage."
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 5

# plotnine world map geometry
world_map = world.copy()   # built-in dataset: has 'name' and geometry cols

# Merge our data onto the map — plotnine uses 'name' column
# We'll match on country name; use alpha_3 as a fallback bridge
world_map = world_map.merge(
    latest_pd[["country","alpha_3_code","sanitation_pct"]],
    left_on="iso_a3", right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

p_map = (
    ggplot(world_map)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="sanitation_pct"), color=WHITE, size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_gradient(
        low="#bbdefb", high=BLUE_DARK,
        na_value="#e0e0e0",
        name="Access (%)"
    )
    + labs(
        title="Global Access to Improved Sanitation Facilities",
        subtitle="Grey = no data  |  Dark blue = near-universal coverage  |  Light blue = low coverage",
        caption="Source: UNICEF, latest available year per country"
    )
    + blue_theme()
    + theme(
        axis_text        = element_blank(),
        axis_title       = element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major = element_blank(),
        panel_border     = element_blank(),
    )
)

p_map
```

The map reveals a stark **North–South divide**. High-income countries in Europe, North America, and East Asia show near-complete coverage (deep blue), while large parts of **Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia** remain light — indicating that fewer than half the population has access to improved sanitation.

---

## 📊 Chart 2 — Bar Chart: Bottom 20 Countries

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-chart
#| fig-cap: "The 20 countries with the lowest sanitation access (%). These are predominantly in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6

bottom20 = (
    latest_pd
    .nsmallest(20, "sanitation_pct")
    .sort_values("sanitation_pct")
)
bottom20["country_label"] = bottom20["country"].str[:22]

p_bar = (
    ggplot(bottom20, aes(x="reorder(country_label, sanitation_pct)", y="sanitation_pct"))
    + geom_col(fill=BLUE_MID, color=BLUE_DARK, width=0.7)
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="sanitation_pct.round(1).astype(str) + '%'"),
        ha="left", size=7, color=BLUE_DARK, nudge_y=0.8
    )
    + geom_hline(yintercept=50, linetype="dashed", color="red", alpha=0.6, size=0.8)
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 55), labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="20 Countries with Lowest Sanitation Access",
        subtitle="Red dashed line = 50% threshold",
        x="Country",
        y="Population with Improved Sanitation (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF indicator data"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_bar
```

Every single country in this list sits **below 50%** — meaning the *majority* of those populations lack adequate sanitation. Countries like **South Sudan, Chad, and Niger** sit below 20%, a figure that would have been unthinkable in a wealthy nation at any point in recent history.

---

## 📊 Chart 3 — Scatterplot: Sanitation vs. GDP per Capita

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Relationship between GDP per capita and sanitation access. Each dot is a country, coloured by region. The line shows the overall linear trend."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6

scatter_df = (
    latest_pd
    .dropna(subset=["sanitation_pct","gdp_per_capita"])
    .query("gdp_per_capita < 80000")   # remove extreme outliers for readability
)

region_colors = {
    "Sub-Saharan Africa":    "#1565c0",
    "South Asia":            "#42a5f5",
    "East Asia & Pacific":   "#0288d1",
    "Latin America":         "#26c6da",
    "Middle East & N. Africa":"#80deea",
    "High Income":           "#b3e5fc",
    "Other":                 "#cfd8dc",
}

p_scatter = (
    ggplot(scatter_df,
           aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="sanitation_pct", color="region"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.7, size=2.5)
    + geom_smooth(
        aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="sanitation_pct"),
        method="lm", color="red", se=True, inherit_aes=False, size=1
    )
    + scale_color_manual(values=region_colors, name="Region")
    + scale_x_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"${v/1000:.0f}k" for v in l])
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Sanitation Access vs. GDP per Capita",
        subtitle="Each point = one country  |  Red line = linear trend",
        x="GDP per Capita (constant 2015 USD)",
        y="Population with Improved Sanitation (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF + World Bank metadata"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_scatter
```

The upward-sloping trend line confirms what we might intuitively expect: **wealthier countries have better sanitation**. However, the relationship is **not perfectly linear**. Several middle-income countries in Latin America and East Asia have achieved high sanitation coverage despite modest GDP, suggesting that **political will and targeted investment matter** — not just wealth.

---

## 📊 Chart 4 — Time Series: Regional Progress Since 2000

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "Average sanitation access by region, 2000–2022. Only regions with sufficient data shown."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

# Attach region to merged data
merged_region = merged_pd.merge(
    latest_pd[["alpha_3_code","region"]],
    on="alpha_3_code", how="left"
)

ts_df = (
    merged_region
    .dropna(subset=["sanitation_pct","region"])
    .query("year >= 2000")
    .groupby(["year","region"], as_index=False)["sanitation_pct"]
    .mean()
    .query("region != 'Other'")
)

region_line_colors = {
    "Sub-Saharan Africa":    "#1565c0",
    "South Asia":            "#42a5f5",
    "East Asia & Pacific":   "#0288d1",
    "Latin America":         "#26c6da",
    "Middle East & N. Africa":"#5c6bc0",
    "High Income":           "#81d4fa",
}

p_ts = (
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="year", y="sanitation_pct", color="region"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=1.5, alpha=0.6)
    + scale_color_manual(values=region_line_colors, name="Region")
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Sanitation Access by Region: 2000 – 2022",
        subtitle="Average % of population with access to improved sanitation facilities",
        x="Year",
        y="Average Sanitation Access (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF indicator data"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_ts
```

Progress **has been made** — every region shows improvement since 2000. But the gaps remain enormous. **High-income countries** plateaued near 99% long ago. **Sub-Saharan Africa**, despite gains, still averages well below 50% as of the latest data. The slope of improvement in South Asia is encouraging, driven largely by India's national sanitation campaigns — but the baseline was so low that enormous work remains.

---

## 📝 Conclusion

The data paints a sobering but important picture:

- **2.4 billion+ people** globally still lack access to basic sanitation (WHO/UNICEF estimates).
- The burden falls **most heavily on Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia**, where poverty, rural geography, and weak infrastructure combine.
- There is a **clear correlation** between GDP and sanitation, yet outliers show policy choices matter.
- **Time trends** confirm progress is real but *insufficient* to meet SDG 6 targets by 2030.

Improved sanitation is not a luxury — it prevents **diarrhoeal disease, child mortality, and the spread of cholera and typhoid**. Every percentage point on these charts represents millions of human lives.

> *The world has the resources. What's missing is urgency.*

---

*Report created using Python (Polars, plotnine) and rendered with Quarto. Data source: UNICEF.*
